sourceMovie.title: The Cell
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit The Cell for the same reason it broke through in 2000: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a science fiction rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Tarsem Singh keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The horror and science fiction blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Twelve Monkeys
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Cell clicked for you, Twelve Monkeys (1995) is a strong next stop: both trade in science fiction and thriller, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Event Horizon
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Cell clicked for you, Event Horizon (1997) is a strong next stop: both trade in horror and science fiction, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Cell?
faq.answer: Try Twelve Monkeys, Event Horizon, Ghostland—each shares DNA with The Cell while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Cell?
faq.answer: The Cell (2000) is best described as Horror, Science Fiction, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Cell part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "The Cell Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
